claim.hypotheses_location: H1: Page 350 under 'THE EFFECT OF BRANDING ON ATTITUDE FUNCTIONS' (We predict the following: H1 ...). H2: Pages 353–354 under 'FUNCTION-MATCHING APPEALS AT THE BRAND AND CATEGORY LEVELS' (We predict the following: H2 ...). H3: Page 355 under 'STUDY 3: INDIVIDUAL DIFFERENCES IN ATTITUDE FUNCTIONS' (We predict the following: H3 ...).

data.source: Laboratory experiments with undergraduate student participants using questionnaires and advertising evaluations
data.sample_size: Study 1a: N=132; Study 1b: N=206; Study 1c: N=139; Study 2a: N=117; Study 2b: N=72; Study 3: N=54; Study 4: N=102
data.notes: Multiple studies using both real and fictitious brands; manipulations of level (category vs brand) and appeal type (utilitarian vs symbolic); measurements on 7-point Likert scales; attitude-function difference scores computed as benefits minus symbolism; analyses include t-tests, mixed ANOVAs, regressions, and correlations.

method.description: Across seven studies, participants evaluated products or advertisements at either the product-category level or the brand level. Studies 1a–1c measured the utilitarian versus symbolic functions associated with products when unbranded versus branded. Studies 2a–2b tested the persuasiveness of utilitarian versus symbolic appeals at the category versus brand level. Study 3 examined individual differences in attitude functions and how they moderate responses to appeals at the two levels. Study 4 correlated real magazine advertisement content (utilitarian vs symbolic focus) with evaluations and the match to product-category functions.
steps: Select products pre-identified as predominantly utilitarian or symbolic at the category level.
Manipulate presentation level: product category (unbranded) versus brand (real, fictitious, or generic 'new brand').
Measure attitude functions with items assessing benefits (utilitarian) and symbolism (symbolic) on 7-point scales; compute difference scores (benefit minus symbolism).
Manipulate persuasive appeal type: utilitarian (costs/benefits) versus symbolic (values/self-expression) tailored to each product.
Collect outcome measures such as purchase likelihood and advertisement evaluations.
In Study 3, measure individual participants’ dominant attitude function for the product category and test appeal congruence effects at category vs brand levels.
In Study 4, code real magazine ads for utilitarian vs symbolic focus, obtain category function ratings, compute match/mismatch indices, and correlate with ad evaluations.
Analyze data using independent-samples t-tests, mixed-factor ANOVAs (level × product type/appeal), linear regressions with interactions, and correlations.
method.models: Independent-samples t-tests; mixed ANOVAs (e.g., 2×2 designs with level and product type/appeal); linear regression with interaction terms (appeal × level × individual attitude function); Pearson correlations.
method.outcome_variable: Primary outcomes include: (a) attitude-function difference score (benefits − symbolism); (b) purchase likelihood ratings; (c) overall advertisement evaluation ratings; (d) correlations between match/mismatch indices and ad evaluations.
method.independent_variables: Presentation level (category vs brand); product type (utilitarian vs symbolic at category level); appeal type (utilitarian vs symbolic; matching vs mismatching to category-level function); brand set (real vs fictitious); individual attitude function (benefit − symbolism difference score, centered).
method.control_variables: Order of product/appeal presentation counterbalanced; brand name sets counterbalanced (e.g., Set A vs Set B); within-study product identity; participant-level random assignment.

results.summary: Branding systematically shifts attitude functions: utilitarian product categories become relatively more symbolic when branded, while symbolic categories become relatively more utilitarian (H1). As a consequence, appeals that match a product category’s dominant function are more persuasive at the category level but lose their advantage—and may even reverse—at the brand level (H2). At the category level, appeal effectiveness depends on an individual’s dominant attitude function (utilitarian vs symbolic), but this function-matching advantage is attenuated at the brand level (H3). With real advertisements, mismatching or more symbolic appeals for branded utilitarian products were often evaluated as favorably or more favorably than matching utilitarian appeals.

metadata.original_paper_title: Branding Alters Attitude Functions and Reduces the Advantage of Function-Matching Persuasive Appeals
